title: Bus 615
summary: A night bus driver in Helsinki waits two extra minutes for one missing regular. Two weeks later, he finds out who saw.
body:
Mr Lehtinen drives the night bus number 615 in Helsinki. He drives from eleven at night to seven in the morning. The bus goes from the central station to a small town called Espoo and back. He has done this for nineteen years.

The bus is almost empty most nights. Sometimes there are two or three people. Sometimes one. Sometimes nobody.

Mr Lehtinen knows the regular passengers. There is the young nurse who finishes at midnight. There is the old man who goes to fish at three. There is the bakery woman from Espoo who starts at four.

At four, the bakery woman gets on. She is sixty-five. She is tired. She has a small bag with bread for her grandson.

One Tuesday in January, she does not come. The bus is empty. Mr Lehtinen waits two extra minutes. He drives on.

The next Tuesday, she is there. She does not say where she was. Mr Lehtinen does not ask.

But before she gets off, she says, 'My sister was very sick last week. She is better now. Thank you for waiting.'

Mr Lehtinen says, 'You saw me wait?'

She says, 'My grandson did. He thought you were her brother.'

Mr Lehtinen smiles. He drives on. The sun is starting to come up.

annotations.meaningVi: ga trung tâm (điểm đầu / cuối tuyến xe buýt hoặc tàu)
lên xe (phương tiện công cộng) — động từ cụm không thể tách
xuống xe (phương tiện công cộng) — động từ cụm, đối lập với 'gets on'
tiếp tục lái / đi tiếp — 'on' nhấn mạnh hành động không ngừng lại
vài phút thêm / phút dôi ra — 'extra' = thêm ngoài dự kiến
gần như trống — 'almost' bổ nghĩa cho tính từ/trạng từ, chỉ mức độ gần đạt 100%
tiệm bánh mì / lò bánh — nơi làm và bán bánh
Cô ấy đã khoẻ hơn rồi — cấu trúc cố định dùng khi ai đó hồi phục sau bệnh ('better' = khoẻ hơn, không phải 'tốt hơn')
mọc lên / xuất hiện (mặt trời) — động từ cụm: sun comes up = mặt trời mọc

grammarNotes.title: 'There is / there are' for introducing people
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi giới thiệu một loạt người hoặc vật, A2 dùng 'There is + danh từ số ít' và 'There are + danh từ số nhiều'. Trong bài: 'There is the young nurse who finishes at midnight. There is the old man who goes to fish at three. There is the bakery woman.' Lặp lại 'There is' khi giới thiệu từng người tạo nhịp văn xuôi rõ ràng. 'There are' đi với số nhiều: 'There are two or three people.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: To introduce a list of people or things, A2 uses 'There is + singular noun' or 'There are + plural noun'. The passage: 'There is the young nurse who finishes at midnight. There is the old man who goes to fish at three. There is the bakery woman.' Repeating 'There is' for each person creates a clear rhythm. 'There are' for plural: 'There are two or three people.'

translationVi:
Ông Lehtinen lái xe buýt đêm số 615 ở Helsinki. Ông lái từ mười một giờ tối đến bảy giờ sáng. Xe đi từ ga trung tâm đến một thị trấn nhỏ tên Espoo và quay lại. Ông làm việc này mười chín năm rồi.

Xe gần như trống vào hầu hết các đêm. Có khi hai ba người. Có khi một. Có khi không ai.

Ông Lehtinen biết các hành khách quen. Có cô y tá trẻ tan ca lúc nửa đêm. Có ông cụ đi câu lúc ba giờ. Có chị bán bánh ở Espoo bắt đầu ca lúc bốn.

Lúc bốn giờ, chị bán bánh lên xe. Chị sáu mươi lăm tuổi. Mệt. Chị có một túi nhỏ đựng bánh mì cho cháu trai.

Một thứ Ba tháng Một, chị không đến. Xe trống. Ông Lehtinen đợi thêm hai phút. Rồi đi tiếp.

Thứ Ba tuần sau, chị có mặt. Chị không kể chị đã ở đâu. Ông Lehtinen không hỏi.

Nhưng trước khi xuống, chị nói: 'Chị tôi rất ốm tuần trước. Giờ chị đã khoẻ. Cảm ơn ông đã đợi.'

Ông Lehtinen nói: 'Chị thấy tôi đợi à?'

Chị nói: 'Cháu tôi thấy. Cháu tưởng ông là anh trai chị tôi.'

Ông Lehtinen mỉm cười. Lái tiếp. Mặt trời bắt đầu lên.

questions.prompt: What hours does Mr Lehtinen drive?
options: From eleven at night to seven in the morning
From seven in the morning to eleven at night
From the central station to Espoo and back
questions.explanation: 'The bus goes from the central station to a small town called Espoo and back.'

questions.prompt: Who are three regular passengers Mr Lehtinen knows?
options: The young nurse (midnight), the old fisherman (3 AM), the bakery woman (4 AM)

questions.prompt: What does the bakery woman carry?
options: A small bag with bread for her grandson

questions.prompt: What happened one Tuesday in January?
options: She did not come; Mr Lehtinen waited two extra minutes and then drove on.

questions.prompt: Why had the bakery woman missed the bus?
questions.explanation: 'My sister was very sick last week. She is better now.'

questions.prompt: What did the grandson think about Mr Lehtinen?
options: That he was the sick sister's brother.
That he was the bakery owner.
He smiles and drives on; the sun is starting to come up.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: A small unannounced kindness can be quietly observed by someone you did not even know was watching.
Buses should always be on time.
Helsinki has too few passengers at night.
Grandsons should not look out windows.
questions.explanation: Mr Lehtinen's two extra minutes were seen — by a child who misidentified him.
questions.hint: Think about who saw the two extra minutes.

cloze.template:
Mr Lehtinen drives the night bus number {{b1}} in Helsinki. He drives from eleven at night to seven in the morning. The bus goes from the central station to a small town called {{b2}} and back. He has done this for nineteen years.

The bus is almost empty most nights. Sometimes there are two or three people. Sometimes one. Sometimes {{b3}}.

Mr Lehtinen knows the regular {{b4}}. There is the young nurse who finishes at midnight. There is the old man who goes to fish at three. There is the bakery woman from Espoo who starts at {{b5}}.

At four, the bakery woman gets on. She is sixty-five. She is tired. She has a small bag with {{b6}} for her grandson.

One Tuesday in January, she does not come. The bus is empty. Mr Lehtinen waits two extra {{b7}}. He drives on.

The next Tuesday, she is there. She does not say where she was. Mr Lehtinen does not ask.

But before she gets off, she says, 'My sister was very sick last week. She is better now. Thank you for {{b8}}.'

Mr Lehtinen says, 'You saw me wait?'

She says, 'My grandson did. He thought you were her {{b9}}.'

Mr Lehtinen smiles. He drives on. The {{b10}} is starting to come up.

blanks.explanation: The grandson thought he was her brother.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Lehtinen waited two extra minutes for one regular passenger and only learned, two weeks later, that a child had seen. Is there a small kindness you have done in private — at work, in your neighbourhood — that turned out to have been quietly seen? What did the witness mistake you for?
